title: The Old Camp-Fire
content:
آ آ آ آ Now shift the blanket pad before your saddle back you fling,
آ آ آ آ And draw your cinch up tighter till the sweat drops from the ring:
آ آ آ آ Weï؟½ve a dozen miles to cover ere we reach the next divide.
آ آ آ آ Our limbs are stiffer now than when we first set out to ride,
آ آ آ آ And worse, the horses know it, and feel the leg-grip tire,
آ آ آ آ Since in the days when, long ago, we sought the old camp-fire.

آ آ آ آ Yes, twenty years! Lord! how weï؟½d scent its incense down the trail,
آ آ آ آ Through balm of bay and spice of spruce, when eye and ear would fail,
آ آ آ آ And worn and faint from useless quest we crept, like this, to rest,
آ آ آ آ Or, flushed with luck and youthful hope, we rode, like this, abreast.
آ آ آ آ Ay! straighten up, old friend, and let the mustang think heï؟½s nigher,
آ آ آ آ Through looser rein and stirrup strain, the welcome old camp-fire.

آ آ آ آ You know the shout that would ring out before us down the glade,
آ آ آ آ And start the blue jays like a flight of arrows through the shade,
آ آ آ آ And sift the thin pine needles down like slanting, shining rain,
آ آ آ آ And send the squirrels scampering back to their holes again,
آ آ آ آ Until we saw, blue-veiled and dim, or leaping like desire,
آ آ آ آ That flame of twenty years ago, which lit the old camp-fire.

آ آ آ آ And then that rest on Natureï؟½s breast, when talk had dropped, and slow
آ آ آ آ The night wind went from tree to tree with challenge soft and low!
آ آ آ آ We lay on lazy elbows propped, or stood to stir the flame,
آ آ آ آ Till up the soaring redwoodï؟½s shaft our shadows danced and came,
آ آ آ آ As if to draw us with the sparks, high oï؟½er its unseen spire,
آ آ آ آ To the five stars that kept their ward above the old camp-fire,

آ آ آ آ Those picket stars whose tranquil watch half soothed, half shamed our sleep.
آ آ آ آ What recked we then what beasts or men around might lurk or creep?
آ آ آ آ We lay and heard with listless ears the far-off pantherï؟½s cry,
آ آ آ آ The near coyoteï؟½s snarling snap, the grizzlyï؟½s deep-drawn sigh,
آ آ آ آ The brown bearï؟½s blundering human tread, the gray wolvesï؟½ yelping choir
آ آ آ آ Beyond the magic circle drawn around the old camp-fire.

آ آ آ آ And then that morn! Was ever morn so filled with all things new?
آ آ آ آ The light that fell through long brown aisles from out the kindling blue,
آ آ آ آ The creak and yawn of stretching boughs, the jay-birdï؟½s early call,
آ آ آ آ The rat-tat-tat of woodpecker that waked the woodland hall,
آ آ آ آ The fainter stir of lower life in fern and brake and brier,
آ آ آ آ Till flashing leaped the torch of Day from last nightï؟½s old camp-fire!

آ آ آ آ Well, well! weï؟½ll see it once again; we should be near it now;
آ آ آ آ Itï؟½s scarce a mile to where the trail strikes off to skirt the slough,
آ آ آ آ And then the dip to Indian Spring, the wooded rise, and strange!
آ آ آ آ Yet here should stand the blasted pine that marked our farther range;
آ آ آ آ And here whatï؟½s this? A ragged swab of ruts and stumps and mire!
آ آ آ آ Sure this is not the sacred grove that hid the old camp-fire!

آ آ آ آ Yet hereï؟½s the ï؟½blazeï؟½ I cut myself, and thereï؟½s the stumbling ledge,
آ آ آ آ With quartz ï؟½outcropï؟½ that lay atop, now leveled to its edge,
آ آ آ آ And mounds of moss-grown stumps beside the woodmanï؟½s rotting chips,
آ آ آ آ And gashes in the hillside, that gape with dumb red lips.
آ آ آ آ And yet above the shattered wreck and ruin, curling higher
آ آ آ آ Ah yes! still lifts the smoke that marked the welcome old camp-fire!

آ آ آ آ Perhaps some friend of twenty years still lingers there to raise
آ آ آ آ To weary hearts and tired eyes that beacon of old days.
آ آ آ آ Perhaps but stay; ï؟½tis gone! and yet once more it lifts as though
آ آ آ آ To meet our tardy blundering steps, and seems to move, and lo!
آ آ آ آ Whirls by us in a rush of sound, the vanished funeral pyre
آ آ آ آ Of hopes and fears that twenty years burned in the old camp-fire!

آ آ آ آ For see, beyond the prospect spreads, with chimney, spire, and roof,
آ آ آ آ Two iron bands across the trail clank to our mustangï؟½s hoof;
آ آ آ آ Above them leap two blackened threads from limb-lopped tree to tree,
آ آ آ آ To where the whitewashed station speeds its message to the sea.
آ آ آ آ Rein in! Rein in! The quest is oï؟½er. The goal of our desire
آ آ آ آ Is but the train whose track has lain across the old camp-fire!
meaning:
This evocative piece by Bret Harte (Francis), titled "The Old Camp-Fire", represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
